title: FT on 'Industry': Inside investment banking’s 'morning meeting' | FT #shorts

transcript.text: I was thinking some puts on the S&P
maybe linked to a call on gold, but
maybe something more housing.
>> With the labor market going gang busters
like it is, it really doesn't make
sense.
>> Let her let her finish.
>> So,
the product I have the traders looking
at includes a put option on the
homebuilders index and a call option on
gold and a sweet kicker credit default
swaps on the home builders too. It's the
ultimate hedge in a tidy suite for
client convenience at margins big enough
to pay us while they own it.
>> I picked that scene because there's a
lot of tension in the room. There's a
big team. They've got competing
interests it seems. What was going on
there, Stuart?
>> All banks have these things called the
morning meeting and the whole idea is to
get everyone in a room, make everyone
nervous and try and drive ideas and to
work out how they're going to make money
that day. So that is actually a classic
setup. They're usually very early in the
morning and ideas are pitched and tossed
out and careers can often be tossed out
as well. The role she has there is to
put together some type of financial
instrument that will be attractive to
the bank's clients. So in that
particular example that had a view
attached to it, i.e. the markets are
going to fall but very often that they
can be view neutral. As long as the
product itself, the little bits that
move inside it generate income um
they're happy to sell them. Harper, the
character we saw there, pitches a very
complex idea. So, if you're a grad
trainee at one of these investment
banks, is that real? Is that the kind of
thing that might happen?
>> You've got a very very sympathetic boss
or maybe no other ideas around the
table. It's possible a graduate uh uh
could pitch something unlikely.
>> Eric is is the boss of the team. What
would that role entail? He sort of paces
the floor with a baseball bat quite
often.
>> I had a boss who paced the floor in a
baseball bat in my time. it wasn't
uncommon. He's there to corral his um
bonuses on the line if his team doesn't
perform. Everybody is under pressure
going all the way up. So those people in
that room work for him and his bonus at
the end of the year will depend on the
ideas and the money they generate.
>> So assuming you're a grad trainee on a
sales floor like this, what would be
your starting salary?
>> So there six figures now for a um grad
coming in. Um VP might get roughly the
same as a bonus going up to director at
250 and above. Managing director 450
salary up to a million.
>> And what do they expect in return?
>> Well, as a grad trainer, you have to do
everything. You have to say yes. You
have to stay as late as they like. You
probably won't do as long hours as
somebody who's putting a corporate deal
together, for example. They work all
night.
>> Do things get easier for graduates? What
can Harper expect?
>> Yes and no. Less dog work. um fewer
people shouting at you and making
example of you. But the pressure doesn't
end just because you get promoted a
couple of times. Indeed, it can get
worse. She'll be working extremely hard.
If some of these big calls deliver, she
might get promoted. And if she does sell
a lot to clients, she'll get a big
bonus.
